search:dotnet-core-microservices-securit.content:
- Use `dotnet new mvc` to create a new .NET Core MVC project.
- Update NuGet packages to their latest versions using `dotnet update`.
- Implement JWT authentication with `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer`.
- Configure CORS in `Startup.cs` with `services.AddCors(options => options.AddPolicy("AllowAllOrigins", builder => builder.AllowAnyOrigin()));`.
- Use HTTPS by configuring Kestrel in `appsettings.json`: `"Kestrel": { "Endpoints": { "Http": { "Url": "http://localhost:5000" }, "Https": { "Url": "https://localhost:5001", "Certificate": { "Path": "<path_to_certificate>", "Password": "<password>" } } } }`.
- Apply the principle of least privilege by configuring role-based access control (RBAC) with `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization`.
- Implement input validation using Data Annotations or Fluent Validation.
- Use dependency injection for managing service lifetimes and dependencies in `Startup.cs`: `services.AddScoped<IMyService, MyService>();`.
- Regularly update .NET Core and all dependencies to mitigate security vulnerabilities.

- Use JWT for secure microservices authentication.
- Implement Ocelot for API gateway and authorization.
- Adhere to the principle of least privilege in microservice design.
- Configure HTTPS server with SSL/TLS certificates.
- Store authorization configuration centrally using a single location.
- Utilize ASP.NET Core Web API Best Practices eBook for guidance.
- Ensure each microservice has only necessary privileges.
- Implement service-to-service authentication and authorization.
- Regularly update dependencies to the latest versions.
